hero.subtitle: THE FRONTIER CAPITÃO
hero.title: Silvino Jacques Archive
hero.desc: Between the bellows of the accordion and the barrel of the rifle, discover the digital memory of a living legend.
hero.map_desc: "On the frontier, respect is the only law. Between the bellows of the accordion and the barrel of the rifle, we forge our history."

bio.label: Brief Biography
bio.title: The Last Gaudério of the Frontier
bio.p1: Silvino Hermiro Jacques was born on February 17, 1906, in São Borja, RS. Son of Leão Pedro Jacques and Máxima de Sant'Anna, he grew up in a traditional frontier family.
bio.p2: He joined the Brigada Militar, but his life changed radically in 1929 after an incident in Santo Ângelo that made him a fugitive. Fleeing North, he served as a loyalist Capitão for his godfather, Getúlio Vargas, in the Revolution of 1932.
bio.p3: Silvino was a man of his word and poetry. His "Décimas" recorded his deeds. He protected families and fought political rivals, establishing a near-parallel state along the border zone between Brazil and Paraguay.
bio.p4: He fell in battle in May 1939 at the Aurora Farm. His story remains alive in oral memory as a folk hero, the last of the frontier gaudérios.
bio.caption: The Gaudério of the Frontier

vozes.title: Voices & Ecos
vozes.quote: If by chance one day I cannot claim victory, let my enemies kill me, and thus full of glory I ask my friends to read my history.

m1.mito_desc: Legend says Silvino had a pact. Bullets hit his chest and fell to the ground.
m1.fato_desc: Documents prove he was wounded several times. He survived by military tactics and protection.

m2.mito_desc: Accounts claim he buried revolution gold in secret locations along the border.
m2.fato_desc: There are no records of fortune. He died leaving very few material goods to his family.

m3.mito_desc: The popular belief that he stole only from the rich to give to the poor of the region.
m3.fato_desc: His actions were driven by local political disputes and clandestine survival along the frontier.

testimonials_static.text: My grandmother used to tell me that he arrived at the farm asking for water, but didn't get off his horse. His gaze was piercing.
They say his body was closed to bullets. The bullet would hit his chest and fall to the ground.
He was a man of his word. If he promised protection, no one touched those settlers.

genealogia.scroll_hint_mobile: Swipe to see where Silvino came from →

contexto.coiteiro_desc: A person who provides shelter, food, and protection on their property to individuals pursued by the law or outlaws.
contexto.invernada_desc: A period of prolonged stay at a farm, typically during winter or the rainy season, for recovery or staging.
contexto.gauderio_desc: A regional term for someone who lives without a fixed destination, often associated with freedom and country life, but sometimes with banditry.
contexto.y1906_desc: Brazil: President Rodrigues Alves consolidates the Old Republic, and the Taubaté Agreement boosts coffee prices.
contexto.y1929_title: Fleeing Rio Grande do Sul after the Santo Ângelo incident.
contexto.y1929_desc: Brazil: The global economic crisis (Wall Street Crash) weakens the coffee oligarchy, paving the way for the 1930 Revolution.
contexto.y1932_desc: Brazil: Civil war in São Paulo against the provisional government of Getúlio Vargas.
contexto.y1939_desc: Brazil: Peak of the Estado Novo (Vargas dictatorship). End of the cangaço and rural banditry. World on the brink of World War II.

footer.desc: PRESERVING THE MEMORY OF THE FRONTIER

decimas.title: Décimas Gaúchas
decimas.author: Silvino Jacques
decimas.text:
The "Décimas" were written by the hands of Silvino Jacques himself, who was an accomplished trovador. Although called Décimas, they were written in Sextilhas (six-line stanzas). In them, he narrates important facts of his life, like a true personal diary.

1
I will tell a story,
That many must know,
But told by someone who didn't see
It is fair one shouldn't believe.
And so that everyone knows
Quite surely I will write.

2
My name I never denied,
And I do not intend to deny,
My name is Sylvino Jacques,
And I never seek evil
It is evil that seeks me
And always shall find me.

3
I have always been pursued
by a bad, sad destiny,
To the point of becoming
A fierce man
And my name being commented
With the reputation of a murderer.

4
This sad fate, from afar
From afar has been following me,
For I believe it is my destiny
That is pursuing me,
Although full of sorrows
I always show myself smiling.

[...Selected excerpt for demonstration...]

112
The weapon of a good gaúcho
Is a good and strong horse,
Fighting risks one's life,
Running avoids death,
That's why I rode the purebred
And headed towards the north.

221
I am a decent gaúcho,
But I was born predestined
To always walk in suffering,
This was already my fate,
From joys and pleasures
Today I live separated.

ibanh.title: The Last of the Bandits
ibanh.author: Brígido Ibanhes
ibanh.text: Definitive work exploring the duality between the hero and the outlaw. Ibanhes dives into police archives and oral memory to reconstruct Silvino's steps.

tese.title: Borders and Myths
tese.author: Maria de Lourdes
tese.text: Academic study on the cultural impact of the Jacques legend on the formation of the South-Mato-Grosso identity and the relationship with the central power during the Vargas Era.
